speaker: interviewer
text: --16th 1995. We're now finishing your hiding in Czernica. How did you know that the war was ending?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was-- the last few weeks, I hid in an abandoned building. In fact, I-- you know, like I ran out of places to-- to go and hide. And I used to come to Keroltchikha and talk to her. And she suggested I should-- you know, people, if they knew I was in the barn, they sort of complained to-- to me when I came down for the food. They said, be afraid, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I spoke to Keroltchikha, and she told me, she says, you know, there is this abandoned house. You could go upstairs in the attic, and-- and you could hide there. That's what I did. In fact, I was sitting there, and it was cold because there was no hay. There was some straw there. But I-- you know, I used to freeze. It was, you know, February, March. No, it was March. The beginning of March. So I used to sit there. And the mice were around me, so I used to just, you know, kick around my feet, the mice should not go on top of me. In fact, when I had a piece of bread, and I would sleep at night, I would put the bread under my head, and they would eat it. The mice would eat it. So I came up with an idea to hang up the piece of bread on a string on-- on the ceiling. And that's how the mice didn't get as often to the bread. They still ate it, but not as much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, while sitting in this attic, there was a opening. There-- there was a straw roof, and there was a hole there. So many-- quite often, I used to get up and peek out and see what's going on in the village. I used to see the peasants walking around and so forth. And Keroltchikha used to update me, sort of, what's going on, you know, that the Russians are getting closer to us, and so forth. So I knew the liberation would be coming soon. So one day, I just stood there and looked out, and I had seen a Russian soldier, I had seen a soldier go, you know, with the-- with the rifle in front of him. One soldier. And I wasn't sure if it's a Russian soldier, but as I watched, then the army followed, so I knew the-- the liberation is here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was like numb there. And I was afraid to go down. I really was afraid to go down down in daylight. You know? And eventually, I went down. And I remember walking out of the village with my mother, like, just walking away. I don't remember how I met my mother. But you knew where she was hiding? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I probably met her [? with ?] Keroltchikha. I assume so. But just the psychological thing that I don't remember that moment when I reunited with my mother, you know, the liberation day. And it was sad, because there was no other member of the family to-- to share the happiness with us, the liberation. But-- I don't-- I remember passing our house, and I remember that, that-- that my mother said we'll probably never come back to this house again, I mean, whatever happens. And we never did get back to the house. I thought, this whole thing is like a dream. Like, I feel I'm talking about it, and-- and sometimes I-- I feel like I wasn't there, just a story I'm telling.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many times during this year period did you see your mother? How many chances did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I seen her a few times. I don't know exactly. In fact, there was another incident I have to tell you, that I came to a peasant for food, and they knew that my mother is in hiding. And they said, another neighbor told me that your mother was shot in the village. And I [? can't-- ?] I said-- the only person-- the only mother, you know, that survived, that I was with, I had hoped to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to Keroltchikha, and I came to Keroltchikha. And she told me that it wasn't true. And then my mother would come to Keroltchikha. Somehow, I probably-- like I said, it seems like a dream. I don't remember these incidents, that I must have told her what day I'm coming or something, that we used to meet at Keroltchikha once in a while. Did other Jews meet at her house too? Or you just knew her well and your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think that there were other Jews that met there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other Jews hiding in town that you found out later?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew of this Shimon Katz. He was hiding. In fact, people told me later that he was sick. He had-- he had cancer. He suffered, and he was so sick, and he died apparently from cancer. He died hiding. There were other Jews in the-- in the woods hiding, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: From your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: From my town? Well, the-- my aunt, one of my aunts, Tema was her name, she moved. Before the war, she moved to Podkamien. But she knew the people in the village. So she-- she had a bunker. By the way, I forgot to mention that. When we were getting ready to go to the ghetto, when the order came out to go to the ghetto, that aunt came, Tema. She lived with her daughter in Podkamien. So she came to us, and she begged us, my mother, my father, we should go-- we shouldn't go to the ghetto. She told us, she says, they will kill us there. Don't go to the ghetto. Let's go. I'm going to the woods. I'm gonna have a hiding place in the woods. And again, my father was-- my father said, that's the order. He sort of followed orders. Now, she came to the village. Her daughter, her name was Duzia. She had a child. He was about a year or two. And she left her child with a peasant in our village. She did pay this woman. The woman had a few children of her own. So she paid her, she should keep the child. And that woman did keep that child. In fact, after liberation, she-- she got in touch with some people from Podkamien. And she begged somebody should pick up the child. So the mother was killed just two weeks before liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Duzia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Duzia was killed with her mother, my aunt, Tema. Duzia was my cousin. They were killed about two weeks before. But her brother survived. Her brother's-- her brother's name was Yaakov. Yaakov Knobel. He survived. In fact, I lived with him after liberation. So he went to pick up the child. And he kept the child with him. And eventually, when they had to leave this part of-- it was under Russian occupation, he gave the child to an orphanage home. And the orphanage home brought the children to Israel, so he lives in Israel. His father was in Russia. His father survived in Russia, and they were reunited in Poland afterwards, and they live in-- in Israel now.

speaker: interviewer
text: He lives in Israel too?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lives in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the child never lived with him? The child lived in-- in-- in an orphanage in Israel after the war? Or with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I don't really know. I lost touch with them for a while. But just about five years ago, I heard-- a woman-- we call it a [NON-ENGLISH]. A woman from Podkamien came to visit from Israel. So she told me that he lives on his own. His father is married a second time. He has a child of-- another child with the second wife. And they all live in Israel. So I don't know details. Somehow I didn't get in touch with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Understand. So now you're liberated, March 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, March 1944, I'm liberated. I meet with my mother, and we walk out of Czernica to Podkamien to try to find some survivors. While walking, it was snowing. It was cold. There was still shooting going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the soldier-- we see the soldier on a horse and-- and driving. And he-- nobody-- no private people were allowed because it was like the front line. He starts here, like, you know, where are you going? And we told him who we are, that we are Jews, we're survivors. And he told us the direction, where we should go. And he'll let us go there. So we came to Podkamien. How do spell that? I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Podkamien. P-O-D-K-A-M-I-E-N. Podkamien. We came to Podkamien, and we met a few Jews, boys and girls, a few families. And we-- there was a-- from a Jewish family, an empty house. We all gathered there. And whenever-- wherever there was a place on the floor, we-- we laid down. We slept over. And people got some potatoes, and they cooked some soup. And we stayed there a few days. And then we seen the Russian army retreating. So we tried to find out what was going on. And they told us we should follow them, because it seems that the Germans are-- you know, the fights are going on, that it's not safe to be there. The Germans might be-- get back here to this town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we marched following the army like for 30 miles or 40 miles. We slept over one night. And it was snowing. It was a very heavy snow storm at the time. And I remember at that time I didn't even have boots. I-- I had-- my feet were tied with rags and galoshes tied down with strings. And that's how I walked with my mother. How would-- how old was your mother at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was in her 50s. Yes, in her 50s.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was she feeling?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was strong like anything. And then we came-- we came to a town of Trzebnica. And there we stopped. We felt it was safe enough. I remember another episode. as we were walking, the army-- they were marching, as they were marching, there were a group of German-- German soldiers that the Russian army captured. What did they call it? The--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were military prisoners. Prisoners of war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Prisoners, yeah. Prisoners of war. So the Russian soldiers were on horses, and the Germans-- soldiers were marching, you know, with their torn coats and so forth. And I was happy to see them in that condition, marching, being caught. We-- so one of the Russian soldiers apparently made a joke, I don't know. You could do with the enemy. He said, do you want the-- the rifle? Do you want to kill a German? And I said, no, I couldn't kill a human being. [LAUGHS] I couldn't do it. Although they killed our family, and they killed us, our souls. But I couldn't do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in Trzebnica back there. So we stayed there in Trzebnica. There was no agency to turn to. There was no place to get food, and we are in a strange town. So there were a few young boys and girls. And we-- we got together to discuss, what do we do? So we decided that we have to go begging again. So now, we couldn't go, a group of people, go begging. People wouldn't give us food. So we decided two people, we divided ourselves, two people. Each one picked one street, and each one went to different directions, begging. And I went once and twice, and I couldn't stand it no more. So I decided that I will ask for work. I come ask for work, and if they have no work, to beg for a piece of bread. And eventually-- I remember, that's-- I made a painting of that. We used to get together in the afternoon, and-- and people used to show-- we used to-- the boys had little sacks, some of them. And everybody showed off who was a better beggar. [LAUGHS] So I went for a few days like that. And eventually, I did get a job. That-- this woman, her father was a doctor. And she was alone with a child. And she needed a babysitter. So I told her that I will babysit, my mother. So she wanted to keep again one of us. And I said, no, we're not separating no more. And I-- we asked her, would she have any other work for us and this, and she did keep us, the two of us. So I was the babysitter. My mother did the laundry wash.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually, there-- there was like a marketplace. And my mother knew how to make soap. She-- like, the brown soap you use here, my mother knew how to make that. So she made the soap, and she took it to the market. And she sold the soap. She gave the money to the woman. You know. So I couldn't take it no more. I think-- thought I was good-- good till now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she brought the money to the woman. She gave her the money. And that's how we stayed there for quite a few months. And then I got sick. I got sick, and I was in bed. So then typhoid-- a lot of the survivors got typhoid. They came out from the woods, and they started eating. Somehow a typhoid epidemic came out. So all these people went to hospitals. And I was in her house, and she called the doctor. She called her father first, and he didn't know what it was. They told me at that time it was typhoid, but I think it was-- I might have gotten a rheumatic fever, something like that. But I know I was sick for a few weeks. I was in bed. So I remember, my friend came to see me. And she was-- she envied that here I have a clean bed. And when the people went to the hospital, their heads were shaved. They shaved off their hair. So I considered myself the lucky one. I had my hair. I didn't have to shave off the hair. And I had a clean bed. So eventually, I got better. And the other people already earned money. They used to bake rolls and go on the market to sell, and various ways they made some money. And I here was working for nothing. But I was so grateful to that-- to that woman that I didn't want to leave her. You know, I took care of the child, and my mother helped her to make money. And we felt we shouldn't leave her flat. Was she Polish, the woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was Russian, I believe. I don't know, for that matter. She-- she spoke Russian somehow. Either she was Ukrainian or Russian, I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: You conversed with her in Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we spoke Ukrainian, but somehow she spoke Russian. She had a Russian name. Kamatchakha was her name. And my friend, like I said, encouraged her-- why would you want to work for nothing here, when you're maybe saving some money, where you could buy a dress already, whatever, a pair of shoes. And I didn't want to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eventually, her son gets sick. And he died. The child died. I don't remember what it was. Many people died because, you know, the medication-- how shall I say, the medical field wasn't that good at the time, and during the war. So when the child died, I asked her, that we want to leave? And she said that that's OK. So then we came to town. And I got a room there. And I used to-- my mother, whatever, you know, I don't remember. I think she did bake rolls for a while. And then I got together with another girl. And I used to travel to Lvov, which was 100 kilometers away. And I used to buy salt there and bring a sack of salt back to Trzebnica. And my mother used to sell it on the market. So I called myself the salt importer. [LAUGHS] And that's how we made a living, you know. And eventually I could get  a pair of shoes and you know, a skirt, whatever. When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: The end of '44?

speaker: interviewee
text: March-- March '44. It was during the summer, during the summer of '44. Then we moved to Dubno. My cousin Yaakov Knobel, Duzia's brother, was in Dubno. So we moved, and we lived with Yaakov in the same apartment. And that time, he brought-- he picked up the child, his nephew, Duzia's son, from the peasant in Czernica, and he lived with us. It was a sad situation, because he tried for his mother, the peasant woman. He didn't know his own mother. And it was said that his mother wasn't alive. And he had-- he was picked up here. He was full of boils, you know, and so sickly, undernourished and so forth. The woman was a poor woman. She hardly could feed her children. So eventually, he-- was very sad. He didn't have a smile on his face. It was difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there in that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly. I think we were there a few months or something. And then we found out that there is-- it was-- now, my cousin worked in an office. So he was-- his-- his earnings were very little. So he couldn't make a living. So what did people do? People used to steal in order to-- could-- that he could-- you know, have enough food or whatever was needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people were anxious to leave because we did-- our dream was to get to America to join the relatives. So we found out that there are transports going to-- to Poland. So we did register. In fact, Yaakov helped us. He sort of-- we followed him. He registered us and this, and we joined his transport to Poland. When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And a lot of people left-- that was in '44, the end of '44. A lot of people left because they felt that you couldn't make a living working, whatever, and if you steal, you wind up in jail. And then you couldn't get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember we had a-- a broken-down couch that we found. We had-- and we were dragging this couch. Pieces were coming out. Some people said, why are you dragging that piece of furniture? [LAUGHS] But we held onto it, just in case we don't have where to sleep, a bed. We dragged that broken piece of furniture.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure who organized those trips. It could be the joint-- I called the joint here, but I never got the answer. They couldn't, you know, trace it back. Some people said it could have been the Israeli Haganah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Bricha. The Bricha.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bricha. We don't know who organized those trips. Then we lived in Poland. And again, I handled there. Again, I couldn't get no work there, so I handled. I remember, again, we lived two families in one room, a room in the kitchen. And this man used to handle, and it was illegal, illegal venture.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- he used to buy some kind of-- alien-- I don't remember. Alien, no, alien something. Currency from the soldiers. He used to buy-- buy that money and somehow sell it. I don't know. But what did he-- he took me for a partner. Why? I was a little girl. And he felt if he's caught, a girl, nobody will search. So I was the courier. That's what you call it? And he used to give me half of his earnings. So that's how I made a living there. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And again, my mother somehow went to handle on the market also. And that's how we made-- and then we tried to write to America. I remember, I had-- somehow I-- I was very optimistic. When we picked our belongings, when we were leaving our home, I took out the address of my aunt, and I sewed it into my jacket. Now, after a year and a half, when I took out that piece of paper, it was trashed. I couldn't read it. But I still sort of, whatever I could, read-- I send a letter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- somehow it was half-- I just had, I think, the-- the address. No, I just wrote New York and not the street number, something like that. They did eventually get the letter. It took a few weeks or whatever, but they did get the letter. So they sent us a package, a care package. So they sent us a pair of working boots and some spaghetti and rice, whatever, a package. So we were just happy to hear-- at that time, you had food already. We had money, so we bought our food. And we were happy to hear, and we were hoping to be able to get to America. We had accounting with the family. Now, we stayed there a few months. Where is this now that you're saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Boynton, Boynton, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-O-Y-N-T-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: There, I remember a girl got married already, so she was [INAUDIBLE], of course. In fact, the boy was younger from her, quite a few years younger. And-- and this girl had a younger sister. But they wanted a-- they were already hassidim, they were living, [INAUDIBLE]. So the hassidim wanted to [INAUDIBLE] off the older girl, not the younger one. So the boy didn't meet the younger sister till the wedding, [LAUGHS] because they wanted to marry off the older girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: So life began already to normalize a little bit. Then again, we felt-- if-- we were told if we would-- the-- the displaced person camps in Germany, we would get to the displaced person camp, we'd have a chance to get to America. And we tried to get to an organized group that took us to Germany, to the displaced person camp. Which one was that? Which camp did you try--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- we were trying to get to any camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, at that point.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that point, just to any camp. We wind up-- we wound up coming to [INAUDIBLE] at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop the tape, and we'll continue.
